body:
## Summary

Claude Code ships with several built-in features that are noisy, unused, or actively counter-productive when Claude Code is driven non-interactively by our `solve` command and inside the Docker image. We should explicitly disable them.

## Why this matters for `solve`

The `solve` command runs Claude Code non-interactively and must behave identically across every host it runs on. Host machines (developer laptops, CI runners, Gitpod, user workstations) may have their own `~/.claude/settings.json` with arbitrary overrides — auto memory enabled, feedback surveys on, telemetry, custom hooks, etc. Any of those leak into a `solve` run and make it non-deterministic.

Therefore `solve` must **verify the effective global Claude Code configuration** before handing off to Claude. It must not write a fresh settings file from scratch (that would clobber the user's own preferences), but instead:

1. **Read** the existing global `~/.claude/settings.json`.
2. **Verify** whether each of the target keys below is already set to the required value.
3. **Override only the target keys** — preserving every other field the user already has. Same for the `"env"` block: merge our required env vars in without dropping user-defined ones.

## Why both env vars AND settings.json (belt-and-suspenders)

Claude Code reads configuration from multiple layers (managed / user / project / local / env). Relying on only one layer is fragile:

- A user's global `~/.claude/settings.json` can *re-enable* something we disabled only via settings.
- An env var can be unset or stripped by a wrapper shell / CI runner / Docker `ENV` override.
- Settings keys and env var names occasionally differ in precedence or scope (e.g. `feedbackSurveyRate: 0` vs `DISABLE_FEEDBACK_COMMAND=1`).

For 100% reliability, every feature below should be disabled **both** via env var **and** via settings.json where a settings key exists. Env vars can live inside settings.json under the `"env"` key — documented at https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings.md#environment-variables — so both controls can be enforced through the same merge step, without requiring a separate shell export.

## Features to disable

### Environment variables

| Variable | Purpose | Source |
|---|---|---|
| `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_AUTO_MEMORY=1` | Disable persistent auto-memory files (`~/.claude/projects/*/memory/`). | https://code.claude.com/docs/en/env-vars.md |
| `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_CRON=1` | Disable `/loop` and `/schedule` skills. | https://code.claude.com/docs/en/env-vars.md |
| `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_TERMINAL_TITLE=1` | Stop rewriting the terminal title. | https://code.claude.com/docs/en/env-vars.md |
| `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_CLAUDE_MDS=1` | Prevent loading any `CLAUDE.md` memory files into context. | https://code.claude.com/docs/en/env-vars.md |
| `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_GIT_INSTRUCTIONS=1` | Remove built-in commit/PR workflow instructions from the system prompt (we inject our own). | https://code.claude.com/docs/en/env-vars.md |
| `DISABLE_FEEDBACK_COMMAND=1` | Disable the `/feedback` command and bug reporting prompts. | https://code.claude.com/docs/en/env-vars.md |

### `settings.json` fields

| Key | Purpose | Source |
|---|---|---|
| `"autoMemoryEnabled": false` | Same effect as `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_AUTO_MEMORY`; settings-level mirror. | https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory.md#enable-or-disable-auto-memory |
| `"spinnerTipsEnabled": false` | Stop showing tips in the spinner while Claude is working. | https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings.md |
| `"awaySummaryEnabled": false` | Disable session recap when returning to terminal after stepping away. | https://code.claude.com/docs/en/interactive-mode.md |
| `"feedbackSurveyRate": 0` | Probability of post-session feedback surveys; `0` disables them. | https://code.claude.com/docs/en/data-usage.md |

## Where to apply

1. **`solve` command** — before invoking `claude`:
   - Read the existing global `~/.claude/settings.json` (create an empty object only if the file does not exist).
   - Verify the four required settings keys and the six required env vars under `"env"`.
   - Override **only** the target keys, preserving all other user-defined fields (including unrelated keys under `"env"`).
   - Also export the six env vars into the Claude subprocess environment (belt-and-suspenders in case the settings merge is ever skipped or the env block is not honored on a given platform).
   - Log the effective configuration at startup so runs are auditable.

2. **`Dockerfile`** — bake the same configuration in:
   - `ENV` directives for all six env vars.
   - Ship a baseline `settings.json` at the image's expected config path containing the four settings keys plus the six env vars under `"env"`, so the container starts in a quiet, minimal, deterministic state even if `solve` is bypassed.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `solve` reads and verifies the global `~/.claude/settings.json`, then overrides only the target keys (four settings + six env vars under `"env"`), preserving all other user fields.
- [ ] `solve` also exports the six env vars into the Claude subprocess environment.
- [ ] `Dockerfile` sets the six env vars via `ENV` and ships a baseline `settings.json` with the four settings keys and the env block.
- [ ] A `solve` run's Claude stdout/stderr no longer contains spinner tips, away summaries, feedback prompts, or auto-memory system-prompt sections.
- [ ] Behavior is identical regardless of the host's existing `~/.claude/settings.json`, and unrelated user settings are preserved after a `solve` run.

## References

- Environment variables: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/env-vars.md
- Settings file: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings.md
- Setting env vars via settings.json: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings.md#environment-variables
- Auto memory toggle: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory.md#enable-or-disable-auto-memory
- Interactive mode (away summary): https://code.claude.com/docs/en/interactive-mode.md
- Data usage (feedback surveys): https://code.claude.com/docs/en/data-usage.md
title: Disable noisy/unused Claude Code features in solve command and Dockerfile
